title: Chapter Thirteen: The Engagement Party
content: The ballroom at Blackwood had never looked more beautiful, and I had never felt more like a thing.

Chandeliers dripped crystal light over two hundred guests — old money, new money, the kind of money that didn't discuss itself but announced its presence in the cut of a suit, the provenance of a handbag, the particular shade of boredom in someone's eyes. A champagne tower caught the glow and threw it back in little explosions of gold. The string quartet played something French and forgettable. The air smelled of gardenias and ambition.

I stood at the center of it in pale gold silk, the diamond on my left hand throwing more light than the chandeliers. Julian's hand rested on the small of my back — warm, certain, proprietary in the way that hands are when they've been given permission to stay.
"You're magnificent," he murmured against my temple, and I could feel his smile without seeing it.
"So are you," I said, and tilted my face up so he could kiss my cheek.

The room approved. I could feel their approval like weather — warm fronts of nods, the bright certainty of flashed teeth. The Vances and the Lintons, united at last. The alliance that would reshape Blackwood's board, its endowments, its future. I was the seal on the contract, and I was performing the role of my life: grateful daughter, delighted fiancée, Academy royalty who had landed exactly where she was supposed to land.
Julian beamed. My father beamed. Mrs. Linton beamed with the thin, practiced smile of a woman who had negotiated this exact outcome over two decades of charity galas and strategic dinner parties.
I beamed back.

Somewhere in the back of my skull, behind the smile and the champagne and the careful choreography of my hand on Julian's arm, I was counting doors. The french doors to the terrace — twelve feet to my left, unlocked. The service corridor behind the bar — twenty feet, staff only but unmonitored after nine. The main entrance — a gauntlet of well-wishers I'd have to smile through. The stairs to the second floor — visible from three-quarters of the room, but the bathroom at the end of the hall had a window that opened onto the east garden.
I catalogued exits the way other people catalogued canapés. It wasn't panic. It was practice. It was the kind of arithmetic you do when you've lived your whole life in rooms you didn't choose.
"Champagne, darling?" Julian pressed a fresh flute into my hand, his fingers brushing mine.
"Thank you." I took a sip. It tasted like celebration. It tasted like champagne. It tasted like nothing at all.
I made it forty-five minutes before I needed the bathroom.
Not because I needed the bathroom. Because I needed two minutes where no one was watching my face.

The hallway was quiet — that specific quiet of party-adjacent spaces, where the music is muffled and the carpet swallows your footsteps. I pushed through the door, locked it behind me, and set down my champagne flute on the marble counter with a click that sounded too loud.
I gripped the edge of the sink and breathed.
One breath. Two. Three.
The door rattled.
"Occupied," I said.
The rattling became pounding. "Cat. It's Sloane. Open the door."

I could have pretended I didn't hear. I could have waited her out. Sloane Ashworth didn't have the patience for sieges. But something in the pitch of her voice — that particular frequency of drunk-and-determined — told me she'd stand there all night if she had to. And the longer she stood there, the more likely someone would notice.
I opened the door.

Sloane swept in like a disaster in evening wear, all crimson lips and smeared eyeliner and the kind of beauty that turned vicious after three glasses of something clear. She locked the door behind her and leaned against it, arms crossed. Her dress was black, severe, wrong for the party in every way that was intentional.
"Congratulations," she said. The word came out slurred and sharp. "You must be so happy."
"What do you want, Sloane?"

"I want to watch your face when you lie to me." She smiled, and it was the worst smile I'd ever seen on her — no malice in it, nothing performative. Just exhaustion. "You're good at it. The lying. I mean, I always knew you were good, but tonight? Tonight you're operating on a level that should frighten you."
"I don't have time for this."

"No, you don't." She pushed off the door and took a step toward me. Up close, I could see she'd been crying — not the dramatic, mascara-track kind. The kind where you don't realize you're doing it until your vision blurs. "You're trading one cage for another, Cat."
"Sloane—"

"At least the Vance cage had familiar bars." Her voice dropped, and for a moment she sounded like someone I might have been friends with in another life. "The Linton cage? You haven't even seen the lock yet."

The words landed in my chest like a stone dropped into still water. I felt the ripples spread outward — through my ribs, my throat, the backs of my eyes. She was drunk. She was cruel. She was right, and that was the worst part, because I couldn't afford to hear it and I couldn't afford to look away.
So I did the only thing I knew how to do.
I looked her dead in the eye and said, "At least my cage has a view."

For a long second, Sloane just stared at me. Then something broke open in her face — not anger, not hurt. Recognition. She laughed, and it was a real laugh, ugly and true, the kind that comes from a place so deep you didn't know it was there until something cracks it open.
She was still laughing when she unlocked the door and walked out.
Julian was standing in the hallway when I emerged.

He took one look at my face — whatever was left there after Sloane's scalpel — and his expression shifted into that particular configuration of concern that I'd come to recognize as Julian's default setting around me. Gentle. Attentive. Always watching, always ready.
"Hey." He stepped closer, close enough that his hand could find my elbow, and it did. Warm fingers, careful pressure. "Are you okay?"
"I'm fine."
He searched my face. Not suspicious — Julian was never suspicious, not of me — but worried. The kind of worried that doesn't push because pushing would be unkind, and Julian was never unkind.
"Sloane?" he asked.
"Sloane."

A small nod. He understood. Or he thought he did. He thought Sloane was a mean girl with a grudge, and I was a bride-to-be with pre-wedding nerves, and this was just a rough moment in an otherwise perfect night. He thought his gentleness was the antidote to her cruelty.
He didn't understand that his gentleness was its own kind of cage.

"Come back to the party with me," he said. It wasn't a demand. It was an offering, extended in a soft voice with an open hand. An escape from the hallway, from Sloane's echoes, from whatever had tightened my jaw. All I had to do was take it.
I took his arm.
The performance continued.
Across the grounds, three hundred yards from the ballroom's glow, the chapel sat in darkness.

Heath had stolen the scotch from Robert Vance's study — the good bottle, the eighteen-year Macallan that was reserved for board members and senators. He'd walked past the party's light, past the cars lining the drive, past the silhouettes laughing behind glass, and he'd come here. To the basement. To the cold.

A single candle threw shadows across the stone walls. The pew where he sat was hard and ancient, carved by hands that had probably built this chapel for people who believed in something. Heath didn't believe in anything right now except the burn of scotch in his throat and the wall he was building.
Every sip was a brick.

Laying it down carefully, one on top of the other, mortar of silence and distance and the particular kind of forgetting that isn't forgetting at all — it's just refusing to look at the thing you can't forget. The girl in the pale gold dress. The ring on her finger. The way she'd said his name the last time they'd been alone, like she was trying to memorize the shape of it.
He took another drink.
The wall grew higher.

He was not crying. He would not cry. Crying was for people who had lost something, and he hadn't lost her — not yet, not officially, not in any way that mattered more than the unofficial ways that already mattered too much.
"She'll come back," he said to the empty pews. His voice was a rasp, dry and rough. "She said she'd come back."
The candle flickered. The chapel said nothing.
He took another drink. Quieter now, talking to the dark like it might answer: "They all say that."
The scotch was half gone. The wall was nearly tall enough to block out the sound of the string quartet bleeding across the grounds — just barely, if he concentrated, if he kept building.
So he kept building.
Back in the ballroom, I was dancing.

Julian led — he always led, not because he was controlling but because it never occurred to him not to, and it never occurred to me to question a thing that had never been otherwise. His hand on my waist. My hand on his shoulder. The quartet playing something waltz-like and the room watching us the way rooms watch couples who have just announced their future.
I was smiling. I was always smiling.
And then I caught the mirror.

It was the large one on the far wall, the antique one with the gilded frame that had hung in this ballroom since before the Academy was the Academy. I'd passed it a dozen times tonight. But this time, mid-turn in Julian's arms, I looked.

The woman in the mirror was wearing pale gold. Diamond at her throat, diamond on her finger. Hair pinned up in an elegant twist. Smile curved just so — warm, genuine, the smile of a woman who had everything she wanted.
I didn't recognize her.

For one second — one terrible, vertiginous second — the face in the mirror was a stranger's. Someone else's eyes, someone else's mouth, someone else's life. I was watching a woman play Catherine Vance, and the performance was so seamless that I couldn't find the seam, couldn't locate the place where the mask ended and I began.
Julian turned me. My feet moved. The music swelled.
I blinked.

The mask snapped back into place. The woman in the mirror was me again — tired, sure, and something glassy behind the eyes that no one else would notice, but recognizably me. Recognizably Catherine Vance, future Linton, the girl with the view.
But the fracture was there. Hairline. Invisible to anyone who wasn't looking for it.
I was looking for it.
The last guest left at midnight.

I stood at the window of my dorm room, forehead almost touching the glass, and looked toward the chapel. I couldn't see it — not from here, not through the trees and the dark and the distance. But I knew where it was. I knew it the way you know where a wound is without touching it.

He was there. I could feel it like pressure in my chest — the same pressure I felt when I held my breath too long. He was sitting in the dark with Robert's stolen scotch, building the wall higher, and every brick he laid was a door closing between us.
I wanted to go to him.

The thought was physical — a pull in my sternum, a weight in my legs, the specific ache of wanting to move and refusing the command. I could picture the path: down the stairs, out the east door, across the lawn, through the trees. Twelve minutes if I walked. Eight if I ran. I knew every step. I knew where the cameras were, and more importantly, I knew where they weren't.
And that was why I couldn't go.

Because going to him tonight, when my engagement was fresh and every eye at Blackwood was still adjusting to the new configuration of power — going to him would be reckless in a way I couldn't afford. Not because we'd be caught. Because if I went to him, I'd take the path I knew, the blind spots I'd memorized, the route I'd been saving.
And saving it meant saving it.

If I went to Heath tonight, I'd burn the exit route I needed for later. The surveillance gaps would be noted. The pattern would be established. The one true path to him — the one that didn't pass through any of my father's watching eyes — would be compromised.
So I stayed.
I pressed my palm flat against the cold glass. The chill bit into my skin, sharp and clean, and I held it there until it hurt. Because the hurt was real, and most of what I'd felt tonight wasn't.
The blizzard was three days away.

I'd seen the forecast that morning — the weather service predictions, the atmospheric pressure maps, the warning flags that hadn't gone up yet but would. Three days until the storm hit Blackwood, and when it did, it would shut everything down. Roads. Power. Surveillance. The entire apparatus of watching and controlling and containing would freeze, and in that freeze there would be a window.

But three days was a long time. Three days was a hundred opportunities to lose conviction. Three days was Heath alone in the chapel, building. Three days was Sloane's voice in my head — *you haven't even seen the lock yet* — and Julian's hand on my back, and the fracture in the mirror that I could still feel like a splinter beneath my skin.
Three days, and the walls were already closing in from every direction.
I pressed my palm harder against the glass.
The cold was the only honest thing left.
